description: In-depth analysis of physical culture and sport: the critical social role of physical education and sport, sport sociology perspectives, sport as a health policy instrument, and the dilemmas of professionalisation.

questions.text: Why is sport as a health policy instrument alone insufficient to address the civilisation-related diseases caused by sedentary lifestyles?
questions.explanation: Sport as an individual activity cannot address structural causes (occupational sitting, transport habits, built environment). Effective health policy requires structural changes: active transport infrastructure, workplace movement opportunities, and equitable access to sport — not only individual responsibility.

options.text: Because sport is only effective in young people
Because structural causes (sedentary occupations, transport, unequal access) cannot be addressed through individual sport participation
Sport is ineffective for improving health
Only competitive sport is effective; recreation is not

questions.text: Sport's value system (fair play, fair competition) is strengthened by the influence of commercial professionalisation.
questions.explanation: False. Commercial professionalisation and the growing financial stakes of media sport create tension with sport's traditional values (fair play, the amateur ideal) — doping scandals, match-fixing, and financial fair play problems all point to the distorting effects of commercial pressure.

options.text: True
False

questions.text: What does the 'Sport for All' movement mean and what social problem does it seek to address?
questions.explanation: The 'Sport for All' movement aims to ensure equal access — sport should not be restricted to the elite and the talented, but accessible to everyone. Key social problems addressed: economic barriers to sport, gender inequality, and the exclusion of people with disabilities.

options.text: It is exclusively about democratising Olympic sport
Ensuring equal access to sport for all social groups — overcoming economic, gender, and disability barriers
Abolishing competition in sport
A funding model for professional sport

questions.text: What sport-sociological explanation can be given for why socioeconomic status strongly determines sport participation?
questions.explanation: According to Bourdieu's theory, sport is tied to cultural and economic capital: high-status sports (golf, fencing, horse riding) reflect the cultural codes of elite groups, while mass sports (football, boxing) are linked to working-class cultural habitus. Sport is not capital-neutral — sport participation reproduces social structure.

options.text: Sport participation is determined exclusively by genetic ability
Sport is tied to cultural and economic capital (Bourdieu) — sport participation reproduces social structure
Socioeconomic status does not influence sporting habits
Sport provides equal opportunities for all social classes

questions.text: The compulsory nature of physical education in schools contradicts sport's voluntary value system.
questions.explanation: False. This is a contested question, but physical education is pedagogical in nature, not competitive sport. Compulsory PE serves a public good function (health, movement, socialisation) that the market cannot provide — making it a pedagogically justified requirement, not a contradiction with voluntary sport.

options.text: True
False

questions.text: What is the primary ethical problem of doping in the context of sport?
questions.explanation: Doping fundamentally undermines the ethical basis of sport — fair competition — by artificially modifying the competitive situation. Secondary problems include health risks, the role-model effect on young athletes, and the spread of a 'dirty competition' culture.

options.text: It is exclusively a health problem; no ethical issue arises
It undermines the basis of fair competition — artificially distorting competitive equality
It only represents a problem in professional sport; it is ethically neutral at the amateur level
Doping is only dangerous to the athlete and does not affect competition

questions.text: How does media sport (television broadcasting, sponsorship) influence the development of football?
questions.explanation: Media sport has an ambivalent effect on football: positive (global reach, funding, talent development opportunities), but also negative (schedules aligned to TV broadcasts, fixture congestion, commercial interests overriding sporting content, athletes as products).

options.text: It has exclusively positive effects — providing funding and viewership
Ambivalent: brings funding and reach, but also schedule distortion, commercial pressure, and the athlete-as-product problem
Media sport does not influence football
Exclusively negative — it completely destroys sport's value system

questions.text: Sport always provides identical health benefits regardless of intensity and type.
questions.explanation: False. Health benefits are intensity- and type-dependent: extreme-intensity sport (e.g., ultra-marathoners, elite athletes) may in some cases carry cardiovascular risks. Moderate-intensity regular exercise shows the best health profile — the dose-response relationship is not linear but U-shaped.

options.text: True
False

questions.text: What is the fundamental idea of Olympism and how is it reflected in the modern Olympic movement?
questions.explanation: Olympism, according to Coubertin's original vision, means sport as an instrument of human perfection and peace between nations — competition is secondary; participation, fair play, and human development are primary. In the modern Olympic movement, this vision is in constant tension with commercial pressure, professionalisation, and political manipulation.

options.text: Olympism is exclusively about improving sporting techniques
Sport as an instrument of human perfection and peace — Coubertin's vision, in tension with commercial pressure in the modern Olympics
Olympism represents the principle of maximum performance
Olympism is a ruleset applying exclusively to amateur athletes

questions.text: Football's global popularity can be attributed exclusively to sporting performance factors.
questions.explanation: False. Football's global spread is the result of complex cultural, economic, and political factors: colonial expansion (British influence), low infrastructure requirements (only a ball needed), cultural identity and national pride, media sport, and global migration processes — sporting performance is only one factor.

options.text: True
False
